coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots as the high-efficient sensor for copper(II) ions

Lou, Jiaying
Bai, Shizhe
Wu, Da
Liu, Baizhan
Ling, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Preparing high photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) by facile and low-cost processes have received considerable research interest. In this paper, high photoluminescent nitrogen-containing CNDs have been synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of the pipe tobacco. The dispersion of these CNDs shows a strong blue luminescent emission with a max peak at 450nm when it is excited at 369nm. The as-made CNDs can sensitively and selectively detect Cu2+ ions compared with Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ ions, which offers a novel sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of the as-made sample; XPS spectra of C1s (b), N1s (c) and O1s (d) of the as-made sample.

(a) Fluorescent spectra of the as-made sample (dot line: excitation, solid line, emission, insert: the photograph of the dispersion with (right) and without UV light 365nm); and (b) the excitation-dependent emissions.

(a) The fluorescent spectra in the presence of different concentrations of Cu2+; (b) selective sensing ability of the as-made sample (λ ex =360nm, F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities at 450nm in the absence and presence of different metal ions, respectively. [M2+]=40μM).

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots as the high-efficient sensor for copper(II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Technical Center, Shanghai Tobacco Group Co. Ltd., Shanghai 200082, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Fudan University, Shanghai 200433, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Y.F. Sha and J.Y. Lou contributed equally to this paper.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High photoluminescent nitrogen-containing CNDs have been synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of the pipe tobacco. Chemical sensing results reveal that these CNDs can sensitively and selectively detect Cu2+ ions compared with other metal ions, which offers a novel sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

► High fluorescent nitrogen-containing carbon nanodots. ► One-step hydrothermal synthesis. ► The pipe tobacco as the starting material. ► Influent factors of synthetic conditions to the fluorescence were explored. ► High sensitively and selectively detect Cu2+ ions compared with other metal ions.

Preparing high photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs) by facile and low-cost processes have received considerable research interest. In this paper, high photoluminescent nitrogen-containing CNDs have been synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment of the pipe tobacco. The dispersion of these CNDs shows a strong blue luminescent emission with a max peak at 450nm when it is excited at 369nm. The as-made CNDs can sensitively and selectively detect Cu2+ ions compared with Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ ions, which offers a novel sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:para: Photoluminescent carbon nanodots (CNDs), as a member of carbon materials, have drawn considerable research interest in recent years [1]. This kind of carbon material contains amorphous or nanocrystalline core with predominantly sp2 carbon. The particle size of CNDs is generally below 10nm and the surface is functionalized by polar organic groups such as carboxylate and/or amino groups. Due to these unique structural properties, these photoluminescent CNDs are more water-soluble and more biocompatible compared with other kinds of nanodots based on heavy metal elements [2]. Therefore, the syntheses of CNDs and their application studies in the fields of bioimaging and biosensing have become hot topics [3].

Methods for the synthesis of the photoluminescent CNDs have been well developed, which generally include laser ablation [4], electrochemical oxidation [5], thermal oxidation [6] and so on [7]. Although these previously reported synthetic approaches are able to generate photoluminscent CNDs, the synthetic processes are thought to be complex and time-consuming [2f]. Therefore, more and more attention has been devoted to develop facile and low-cost processes to prepare photoluminescent CNDs. Recently, high fluorescent CNDs have been synthesized by using watermelon peels as the carbon source with only two steps [8]. Hydrothermal treatment method is another simple way to prepare CNDs. Using the glucose as the carbon source, intrinsically fluorescent CNDs have been hydrothermally synthesized [9]. Based on this work, high efficient fluorescent nitrogen-containing CNDs have been reported recently by using glucosamine hydrochloride as the starting material [10]. However, most of these studies focus on the fluorescent properties and the bioimaging or biosensing abilities, very few CNDs have been prepared for chemical sensing, which limits their application.

In this paper, high fluorescent nitrogen-containing CNDs have been prepared by the hydrothermal method and used as a kind of chemical sensor for metal ions. The pipe tobacco was chosen as the starting material, because it contains many kinds of nitrogen compounds, which can serve organo-nitrogen source. During the hydrothermal process, the formation of CNDs and their surface passivation take place simultaneously, leading to the formation of fluorescent nitrogen-containing CNDs. Chemical sensing results indicate that these CNDs can sensitively and selectively detect Cu2+ ions compared with that of Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ ions, which offers a novel sensing platform for the detection of Cu2+ ions.

All chemicals were purchased from commercial sources and used as received. The pipe tobacco was obtained from Shanghai Cigarette Factory and used as the source of CNDs. In a typical synthesis procedure: 0.5g pipe tobacco and 10mL deionized water were added into a teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave (15mL) and then heated at 180°C for 8h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was collected by filtration with a 0.2μM filter membrane, then followed by centrifugation at 12,000rpm for 30min to further remove the large particles. To obtain the solid samples, the aqueous solution of CNDs was concentrated and then six equivalents (volume ratio) of acetone were added to precipitate the CNDs.

TEM was carried out on Tecnai G2 Spirit with the accelerating voltage of 300kV. IR spectra were measured on a Nicolet 470 FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 4000–400cm−1 with KBr pellets. XPS experiments were carried out on a RBD upgraded PHI-5000C ESCA system (Perkin Elmer) with Mg Kα radiation (hν =1253.6eV). Binding energies were calibrated by using the containment carbon (C1s =284.6eV). Photoluminescence measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with monochromator (resolution: 0.2nm) and 150W Xe lamp at room temperature. The sensing experiments [11] for metal ions were carried out as following: 200μL of the obtained dispersion of CNDs was diluted to 2mL with deionized water, the excitation spectrum was set to be 369nm and the range of emission was set to be 380–700nm with a scan speed of 240nm/min, EX and EM slit: 10nm, PMT voltage: 400V. The quantum yield was directly measured by HORIBA (Jobin-Yvon) FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer equipped with Quanta-φ integrating sphere accessory by excited at 369nm at room temperature. The slit widths were set to be 2.0nm and detector parameters were optimized, but not saturate the excitation Rayleigh peak.

As shown in Fig. 1a and Fig. S1, the TEM images indicate that the as-made particles are well dispersed with the particle size below 5nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image indicates that these obtained particles are amorphous, which has been further confirmed by the PXRD pattern (Fig. S2). This is different from the carbon nanodots obtained from graphite, which generally has the lattice spacing [2e]. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3) shows that the peaks around 3427cm−1 corresponding to the stretching mode of OH group. The peaks around 1701, 1517, 1282 and 1117cm−1 indicate the presence of carboxylic groups [8,11]. The peaks around 2968 and 2935cm−1 suggest the existence of alkyl groups on the surface of the as-made nanoparticles. These adsorption bands have not been previously observed [1]. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1b–c and Fig. S4) shows three peaks at 284.4, 399.6, and 532.2eV, which are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, confirming the presence of O and N atom in the as-made nanoparticles. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1b) gives three peaks at 284.6, 286.8, and 288.3eV, corresponding to binding energy of CC, CN and CO, respectively [11]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows two peaks at 531.8 and 532.8eV, which can be attributed to CO and COH/COC groups, respectively. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1c) shows one peak at 399.6eV, which can be assigned to the CNC/N(C)3 [12]. All the characterization results illustrate that these nanoparticles obtained by the hydrothermal treatment are amorphous nitrogen-containing CNDs, which are slightly different from the previously reported CNDs [13].

Photoluminscent studies were then carried out on these carbon nanopartilces. As shown in Fig. 2a, upon excited at 360nm, the dispersion shows a strong fluorescent emission band at 450nm, indicating the hydrothermally synthesized nanoparticles are fluorescent. The photograph of the dispersion (insert) shows bright blue color under UV light (365nm), further directly confirming the strong blue fluorescent emission. Furthermore, the influence factors of synthetic conditions (concentration, temperature and pH) to the fluorescence were also investigated (Fig. S5). With lowering the hydrothermal temperature or increasing the concentration of the pipe tobacco, the intensity of the fluorescent emission gradually turns weak. When using KOH to adjust the pH value, the intensity of fluorescent emission also turns weak. These results suggest that the intensity of the fluorescent emission is strongly dependent on the hydrothermal conditions. Excitation wavelength-dependent feature is one of the criteria to identify whether the as-made carbon particles are CNDs or not [1]. In our studies, when the excitation spectrum is varied in the range of 310–430nm, the peak of the emission bands synchronously varies from 425 to 515nm (Fig. 2b). The excitation wavelength-dependent feature of these CNDs can be attributed to the slightly difference in particles sizes as well as the distribution of different emissive sites on each CNDs [1]. Furthermore, the quantum yield of these CNDs was measured to be 3.2%, which are comparable to these reported CNDs obtained by different carbon sources [8–11]. Therefore, the fluorescent properties further illustrate that these nanopartilces are a kind of CNDs.

Considering the present of organo-nitrogen groups and carboxylate groups around the surface of CNDs as well as the efficient fluorescent property, detection of metal ions was then carried out. As seen in Fig. 3 , with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+ ions in the dispersion, the intensity of the fluorescent emission synchronously turns weak, indicating the quench effect of Cu2+ ions to the fluorescent CNDs. This phenomena could be ascribed to the electron or energy transfer from CNDs to Cu2+ ions [14]. Sensitivity of the CNDs to the Cu2+ ions was then carefully investigated with the concentration in the range of 0–40μM. The quenching data was analyzed with Stern–Volmer method. As shown in Fig. S6, in low concentration, it fits the linear Stern–Volmer equation with the K sv constant of 2.76μM−1 (adj. R 2 =0.99), illustrating a quencher inhibits either a photochemical reaction or a photophysical process by a single reaction (Static quenching process). Based on the results, the detection limit for Cu2+ was estimated to be 0.01μM, which is lower than that of nanodots based on heavy metal ions such as CdS [15a] and ZnS [15b] QDs. With further increase of the concentration of the Cu2+ ions, it does not fit the static quenching process any more. These results indicate that both dynamic and static quenching process may occur at high concentration [16]. Then, as a comparative study, Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ ions were also studied. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S7, there is no tremendous decrease in fluorescent intensity by adding other metal ions (40μM) into the dispersion. These observations indicate that the fluorescent emission of the nitrogen-containing CNDs is not only sensitive to Cu2+ ions but also selective to detect the Cu2+ ions.

In summary, CNDs has been successfully synthesized by hydrothermal treatment of the pipe tobacco and completely confirmed by TEM, XPS and photoluminscent studies. Compared with carbon nanodots reported previously, this method is simple without any complex or post-treatment procedures. Chemical sensing results indicate that these CNDs can efficiently detect Cu2+ ions compared with Ca2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+ ions, which offers a novel sensing platform for selective sensing of Cu2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.materresbull.2012.12.010.
